---
title: "Carolina Willow (Salix caroliniana)"
slug: "salix-caroliniana"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475893375/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Carolina Willow"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Malpighiales"
tax_family: "Salicaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Malpighiales", "Salicaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/475893375/medium.jpeg" alt="Carolina Willow" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/coreytcallaghan">
coreytcallaghan</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264988700">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Malpighiales
-   **Family:** Salicaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=125683)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida.

## Natural history notes

A small tree or large shrub characterized by its narrow, lance-shaped leaves and smooth gray bark. In Florida, it is commonly found in wet habitats such as swamps, marsh edges, and along rivers and lakes. It flowers in early spring, producing catkins that provide an important early-season resource for pollinators, and its dense growth offers habitat for wildlife.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/125683-Salix-caroliniana)
-   [Local profile](https://www.fnps.org/plant/salix-caroliniana#gsc.tab=0)
